An agent-based model (ABM) is a computational framework used to simulate the actions and interactions of autonomous agents, with the goal of assessing their effects on the system as a whole. In this case, the ABM is designed to replicate and understand the adoption behaviors of milk consumption by the British public from 1974 to 2005, focusing on the shift from whole milk to skimmed and semi-skimmed milk. The model aims to explore how perception, habits, and social influence affect an individual's decision-making process regarding milk choice. The main outcome of the ABM is the average weekly consumption of skimmed and semi-skimmed milk per agent (whole milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skimmed milk, skimmed and semi-skim  in driving the adoption of alternative milk types. The rapid increase in consumption suggests that interventions targeting perception changes and social norms can be highly effective. The fluctuations and cyclical patterns indicate that continuous efforts may be needed to sustain high levels of adoption. Understanding these dynamics can help in designing strategies to promote healthier and more environmentally friendly milk choices.   variability around the mean, indicating that while the overall trend is increasing, there are fluctuations in the rate of increase. The rolling Mann-Kendall test results confirm a consistent increasing trend across all windows, with significant p-values and positive slopes, reinforcing the steady rise in deviation scores. The inflection points at indices 7, 12, and 28 suggest periods where the rate of increase changes, potentially indicating shifts in the underlying dynamics of agent decision-making. The oscillation indices highlight periods of minor fluctuations within the overall increasing trend. For decision-makers, these trends imply that over time, the factors influencing agents' milk choices lead to greater diversity in their decision-making processes. This increasing divergence could be due to the cumulative effects of social influence, habit formation, and cognitive dissonance, which may cause agents to deviate more significantly from one another in their preferences and choices.
